paragraphs.text:
## Metal–Protein Binding in NIST SRD-46

### Direct Answer: Proteins Are Not in SRD-46

After exhaustive Phase 0–3 searching, **NIST SRD-46 contains no data on intact proteins**. Every protein ligand search returned zero results:

| Protein Searched | SRD-46 Result |
|---|---|
| Transferrin | ❌ Not found |
| Lactoferrin | ❌ Not found |
| Human Serum Albumin | ❌ Not found |
| Metallothionein | ❌ Not found |
| Ferritin | ❌ Not found |
| Casein | ❌ Not found |

paragraphs.text:
### What SRD-46 *Does* Contain: Protein Building-Block Ligands

The database covers **5,750 ligands** across 30+ classes. The classes most relevant to protein metal-binding sites are well-represented:

| Ligand Class | # Ligands in SRD-46 | Protein Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Amino Acids** | 297 (5.2%) | Gly, His, Cys, Met, Asp, Glu — direct protein residues |
| **Peptides** | 157 (2.7%) | Dipeptides modeling protein backbone coordination |
| **Polypeptides** | 98 (1.7%) | Longer chain models |
| **Tripeptides** | 66 (1.2%) | e.g., Gly-Gly-Gly, His-containing tripeptides |
| **Pyrroles/Azoles** | 134 (2.3%) | Imidazole — histidine side-chain mimic |
| **Catechols** | 66 (1.2%) | Tyrosinate — key Fe³⁺ donor in transferrin |
| **Thio-carboxylic acids** | 71 (1.2%) | Cysteine/thiol coordination |
| **Hydroxamic acids** | 74 (1.3%) | Siderophore/iron-binding models |

paragraphs.text:
### Why Proteins Are Excluded by Design

SRD-46 is a **critically evaluated thermodynamic database** for aqueous solution equilibria of small, discrete coordination compounds. Proteins are excluded because:

1. **No single log β is definable** — proteins have multiple, heterogeneous binding sites that cannot be collapsed into one equilibrium constant
2. **Cooperativity & allostery** — binding is not a simple stepwise equilibrium amenable to the database's formalism
3. **Measurement heterogeneity** — protein Kd values come from ITC, fluorescence, and equilibrium dialysis, not the standardized potentiometric titrations used in SRD-46
4. **Macromolecular scope** — SRD-46 is explicitly scoped to small inorganic and organic ligands

paragraphs.text:
### Chemistry Insight 🔬

Transferrin binds Fe³⁺ with K ~ 10²⁰ M⁻¹ using **two Tyr (catecholate-like donors), one His (imidazole), one Asp (carboxylate), and one synergistic carbonate**. Every one of these donor-atom types is represented in SRD-46 through small-molecule analogs — catechols, imidazoles, amino acids, and carboxylates. Studying the stability constants of Fe³⁺ with catechol (log β ~ 20 for tris-catecholate complexes) or histidine in SRD-46 gives powerful mechanistic insight into why transferrin achieves such extraordinary iron affinity. In this sense, SRD-46 is an excellent **molecular toolkit** for understanding protein–metal interactions at the individual residue level, even though it does not list the proteins themselves.

paragraphs.text:
### Recommended Alternatives for Protein–Metal Data

| Resource | Content |
|---|---|
| **MetalPDB / BioLiP** | Metal coordination sites in protein crystal structures |
| **BRENDA** | Metalloenzyme kinetics and cofactor data |
| **NIST SRD-74** | Biochemical thermodynamics for enzyme reactions |
| **Primary literature** | ITC/fluorescence studies on transferrin–Fe³⁺, albumin–Cu²⁺, etc. |

> **Source:** NIST SRD-46 (Critically Selected Stability Constants of Metal Complexes) — confirmed by systematic search across all protein ligand names and ligand class distribution analysis of all 5,750 ligands in the database.
